g.data-narration: Priority queues serve elements by priority, not insertion order. This educational diagram covers types, operations, implementations, and applications.
g.text: An Essential Abstract Data Type for Algorithm Design

g.data-narration: A priority queue serves elements by priority, not insertion order. The element with highest or lowest priority dequeues first.
g.text: An abstract data type where each element has a priority value.
Elements are served by priority, not by insertion order (FIFO).

g.data-narration: Regular queues follow FIFO order - first in, first out. Priority queues ignore insertion order and serve by priority instead.
Min-priority queues serve smallest value first (deleteMin), while max-priority queues serve largest value first (deleteMax).
The priority queue ADT defines core operations: insert adds elements, deleteMin/Max removes highest priority, peek views without removing.
Unsorted array: O(1) insert by appending at end, but O(n) delete requiring linear scan to find min/max element.
g.text: Best when: More inserts than deletes

g.data-narration: Sorted array maintains order by priority: O(n) insert requiring shifts, but O(1) delete from end. Stores in descending order for min-PQ.
g.text: Best when: More deletes than inserts

g.data-narration: Sorted linked list maintains priority order: O(n) insert traversing to position, O(1) delete from head. Efficient for peek operation.
Unsorted linked list provides O(1) insert at head, but O(n) delete requiring full scan. Mirror trade-off of sorted version.
This table summarizes time complexities: binary heap provides O(log n) for both insert and delete - the best general-purpose performance.
For min-PQ with sorted array, store in descending priority order so smallest ends at index size-1 for O(1) deletion from end.
g.text: Key Trick: Sorted Array for Min-PQ
Store elements in DESCENDING priority order (largest → smallest)
This places smallest priority at index [size-1] for O(1) deleteMin!

g.data-narration: Dijkstra's shortest path algorithm uses min-priority queue to always process the vertex with smallest distance first, guaranteeing optimal paths.
Huffman coding uses min-priority queue to combine smallest frequencies. CPU scheduling uses max-priority queue for highest priority processes.
Priority queues also power A* pathfinding, event-driven simulations, Prim's MST, and network routing for packet prioritization.
When priorities are equal, use FIFO tie-breaking (insertion order) or arbitrary. FIFO creates stable priority queues.
Binary heap provides O(log n) for both insert and delete with O(1) peek - optimal balance for general-purpose priority queues.
g.text: O(log n) for both insert and delete
O(n) build time (heapify in linear time!)

g.data-narration: Priority queues serve by priority not FIFO order. Min-PQ uses deleteMin, max-PQ uses deleteMax. Implementation choice depends on operation frequency.
